mitigation_mechanism: Farm ponds, check dams, and percolation tanks capture seasonal rainfall for use during dry spells, extending the growing season and providing supplemental irrigation at critical crop growth stages. Contour bunds retain in-situ soil moisture, reducing crop stress during within-season dry periods.
caveats: Effectiveness constrained by storage capacity vs catchment area. Sediment management essential — siltation can reduce storage by 20-40% within 5 years without desiltation.
justification: Jha et al. (2014), Singh et al. (2016), Bera & Mukhopadhyay (2023) and the majority of the 85 WH stocktake papers address mixed rainfed systems in South Asia and Africa where drought is the primary water stress.

mitigation_mechanism: In semi-arid systems with 300-700mm rainfall concentrated in 2-4 months, water harvesting is the dominant strategy. Check dams, gully plugs, and micro-watershed systems harvest flash runoff. Groundwater recharge from percolation tanks sustains livestock watering through the 7-9 month dry season.
caveats: Flash runoff events are highly variable — design standards must account for inter-annual variability. Structures sized for mean rainfall will overflow in high-rainfall years and be insufficient in low-rainfall years.
justification: Haile & Suryabhagavan (2018), Mahmoud et al. (2014), Mbilinyi et al. (2007), and Zheng et al. (2023) all address semi-arid agropastoral contexts in Africa and Asia where water harvesting is the primary livelihood resilience strategy.

mitigation_mechanism: Check dams and farm ponds intercept peak runoff flows, reducing volume and velocity reaching downstream channels. Contour bunds and bench terraces slow surface flow, extending time of concentration and reducing peak discharge. At watershed scale, a structure network can attenuate flood peaks by 20-40% for moderate-frequency events.
caveats: Effect on extreme flood events (>50-year return period) is limited. Structures designed for WH are too small to meaningfully attenuate catastrophic floods. Poorly sited structures that overtop can increase downstream risk. Terencio et al. (2017) flag floodplain locations as hazardous for RWH.
justification: Abd-el-Kader et al. (2023), Ahmad et al. (2024), and Al-Hasani et al. (2023) explicitly target flood risk reduction as a RWH co-benefit.

mitigation_mechanism: Bench terraces and contour bunds retain rainfall in-situ on sloped highland terrain, dramatically reducing surface runoff and increasing soil moisture. Hillside ponds and check dams in valley bottoms capture dry-season flows for supplemental irrigation of high-value vegetable crops.
caveats: Terracing is labour-intensive to construct and maintain. Terrace failure due to burrowing animals or storm events can cause severe erosion. Slope angle is a hard constraint — terracing above 45 degrees is unsafe and uneconomic.
justification: Kumar et al. (2018) demonstrate morphometric analysis for WH structure siting in the Himalayan Lower Kosi basin. Amdihun et al. (2014) and Worku et al. (2020) cover Ethiopian highlands. Jhariya et al. (2018) prioritise highland watershed management.

mitigation_mechanism: Small reservoirs, pans, and earth dams in pastoral landscapes store seasonal rainfall for livestock watering throughout the dry season. Groundwater recharge from percolation tanks sustains boreholes and hand pumps. Strategic placement along seasonal watercourses maximises capture of flash runoff events.
caveats: Large communal water points can concentrate livestock, causing overgrazing and degradation around structures. Governance arrangements for communal water points are critical to sustainability.
justification: Mahmoud et al. (2014) map RWH potential in Somali and Borena pastoral regions. Mbilinyi et al. (2007) and Haile & Suryabhagavan (2018) cover pastoral contexts in Tanzania and Ethiopia. Shalamzari et al. (2019) address RWH for wildlife and livestock.

mitigation_mechanism: Gully plugs and check dams intercept surface runoff before it concentrates in low-lying areas, reducing waterlogging in valley bottoms. Where siting accounts for drainage patterns, structures can intercept runoff before it inundates cropland.
caveats: Waterlogging mitigation is highly dependent on precise siting relative to drainage topology. Structures in naturally waterlogged floodplain areas risk failure. This application is secondary to drought mitigation in most stocktake papers.
justification: Several stocktake papers include waterlogging risk as a siting constraint. Terencio et al. (2017) flag flood/waterlogging risk as a suitability constraint. Indirect evidence from watershed management literature.

mitigation_mechanism: Water stored in farm ponds from RWH can supplement canal irrigation during heat stress periods when evapotranspirative demand peaks. The primary relationship is indirect — stored water reduces temperature stress by maintaining adequate soil moisture, not by directly cooling crops.
caveats: Direct heat stress mitigation from RWH is limited. Primary benefit is maintaining water supply reliability. Paddy systems are typically water-sufficient; RWH value is greatest at the tail end of irrigation schemes.
justification: Indirect evidence from South Asian paddy contexts. Not directly addressed as a heat stress intervention in the 85 WH stocktake papers — rated from expert opinion.

mitigation_mechanism: Water harvesting structures do not directly address fire risk in dryland cereal systems. There is no mechanistic pathway by which check dams or farm ponds reduce wildfire ignition or spread at landscape scale.
caveats: Fire risk management requires fuel management approaches. Including fire as a benefit of RWH would misrepresent the evidence base.
justification: No papers in the 85-paper WH stocktake identified fire mitigation as a benefit of water harvesting and conservation. This none rating reflects absence of evidence.
